extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a simple insulated thermal incubator designed for temperature regulation within a styrofoam box, regardless of the box contents. It contains a heatbed and fan for heat distribution, two internal temperature sensors, an external temperature sensor, and is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. A low‑level driver abstracts sensor and actuator communication, forwarding data to a RabbitMQ communication server. The system operates in a controlled laboratory environment as a cyber‑physical platform, where the cyber part comprises the controller, communication server, and software, while the physical part includes the heatbed, fan, sensors, and the insulated container.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers real‑time monitoring of the incubator’s internal and ambient temperatures by ingesting sensor data through the low‑level driver. It provides predictive simulation services based on analytic models (Model A and Model B) that compute the temperature evolution inside the insulated box. The Twin includes model calibration functionality, where the collected data feed back into the models to refine their free parameters. Control services are supplied by the bang‑bang controller running on the Raspberry Pi, which translates temperature measurements into heating (heatbed) and cooling (fan) actuation commands.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on several open‑source components: a Raspberry Pi (embedded controller), RabbitMQ (message broker for command and data exchange), and a custom low‑level driver that abstracts sensor and actuator communication. The entire hardware and software specification, including model code and calibration scripts, is hosted on a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator). The analytic temperature models (Model A and B) are derived from the first‑principles heat‑equation and implemented in lightweight code executed on the Pi, using the Raspberry Pi’s GPIO interfaces for actuator control and sensor reading.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin architecture comprises three core components: a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator communication and publishes temperature data; a controller running on a Raspberry Pi that implements a bang‑bang temperature regulator; and a RabbitMQ communication server that provides AMQP‑based message exchange between the driver and controller. The architecture is loosely coupled through the broker, with data routed as messages; coordination is achieved by the driver interpreting the controller’s commands and vice versa.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Data is gathered from the physical twin via two internal and one external temperature sensor, which are read periodically by a low‑level driver and published to a RabbitMQ message broker. The Digital Twin employs two analytical models that predict the internal temperature: Model A assumes all electrical power is converted to air heating; Model B relaxes that assumption to account for heatbed heat loss. The models are calibrated using a least‑squares optimisation routine that adjusts the free parameters of the differential heat‑equation models. The data–model pipeline therefore consists of sensor data ingestion through RabbitMQ, model simulation based on the heat‑equation, and an optimisation algorithm that updates the model coefficients, enabling real‑time prediction and control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a Raspberry Pi‑controlled heatbed (heater) and a fan. The heater implements a bang‑bang sequence: it is turned on for a duration H when the measured temperature falls below the lower limit, and off for duration C while the temperature is above the upper limit. The fan is activated on every heating cycle to distribute heat uniformly inside the insulated styrofoam box; its actuation is binary (on/off). Remote control is via RabbitMQ messages from the controller to the low‑level driver. Safety constraints are enforced by a safe protocol in the low‑level driver that limits current draw and ensures heatbed shutdown if temperature exceeds an upper safety threshold.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises two thermistor‑based temperature sensors located inside the styrofoam box and one ambient temperature sensor mounted externally. Each sensor delivers a voltage proportional to temperature with an accuracy of ±0.5 °C. The low‑level driver polls the sensor bus at a fixed sampling interval and forwards the readings to the RabbitMQ broker, where the Digital Twin ingests the data as a time‑series. The data are transmitted at the polling rate, typically in the order of seconds per reading, and are associated with timestamps for event monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The model fidelity is governed by four key assumptions: (1) the temperature inside the box is spatially uniform thanks to active fan circulation (Assumption 1); (2) the box walls do not accumulate significant heat that would affect the air temperature (Assumption 2); (3) the specific heat capacity of the air is treated as constant across the operating range (Assumption 3); and (4) the majority of electrical power is assumed to be converted to heat energy, thus the heatbed’s own heat absorption and fan heating are neglected (Assumption 4). Model validity is assessed through calibration experiments – sensor data are compared against the predictions of Model A and Model B, and a least‑squares optimiser adjusts the free parameters until residuals are minimized. Anomaly detection is integrated via a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature; deviations from expected residuals trigger alerts. Validation also includes experiments verifying the uniform temperature distribution, verifying the negligible wall heat effect, and measuring sensor accuracy. All models and data undergo version control and are stored in the repository for reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The physical twin is a thermal incubator composed of a styrofoam container, a heatbed, a fan, and three temperature sensors (two inside the box and one outside). A low‑level driver running on a Raspberry Pi periodically reads the sensor values. Each measurement is packaged into a message and published to a RabbitMQ broker using the AMQP publish/subscribe mechanism. The digital twin subscribes to these topics, aggregates the telemetry, and forwards it to downstream services such as dashboards or analytical models. No additional preprocessing is mentioned beyond the message packaging, and data integration is achieved through the broker’s routing of telemetry to the DT services.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The virtual Twin issues control commands back to the PT through the same RabbitMQ broker. The controller component listens on a command queue for “set‑state” messages that specify actuator targets for the heatbed and fan. Upon receiving a command, the controller executes a bang‑bang policy: the heatbed stays ON for the configured heating duration H until the measured temperature rises to the upper limit UL, then the fan drives for the waiting duration C before the heatbed shuts off until the temperature drops to the lower limit LL. The low‑level driver enforces a safe protocol, validates command payloads against the actuator state machine, and, after actuating the devices, publishes the confirmation (e.g., actuator status and timestamps) back to RabbitMQ. Thus the DT’s control logic is continuously validated by feedback data from the PT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between the physical and digital twins uses a RabbitMQ broker that implements the AMQP messaging protocol over TCP/IP. Telemetry and control messages are exchanged over the local network between the low‑level driver, controller, and other DT services. The broker’s message persistence and routing features provide basic reliability; bandwidth, latency, and security requirements are not specified explicitly in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT stack is hosted on‑premises on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. The Pi runs the RabbitMQ broker, the controller process, and the low‑level driver. Storage of telemetry and model data is performed locally on the Pi’s filesystem or forwarded to external storage; the documentation does not detail the exact storage architecture. The deployment is a single-node, non-clustered edge deployment suitable for laboratory or small-scale field environments.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Conceptual Design & Modelling – A thermal incubator model is built from the physical laws using the energy equation Q = cmΔT and four simplifying assumptions (A–D). Two dynamic models are derived: Model A (all assumptions) and Model B (excluding Assumption 4 to capture heat‑bed inertia). Physical Twin Deployment – Hardware (styrofoam enclosure, heatbed, fan, two inside‑box temperature sensors, one outside sensor) is assembled on a Raspberry Pi and controlled by a bang‑bang controller parameterised by LL, UL, H and C. Digital Twin Integration – Model B is wrapped in a calibration routine that updates model parameters using a non‑linear least‑squares solver. Data from the PT are streamed through RabbitMQ by the low‑level driver and reused by the DT to keep the simulation synchronised. Runtime Monitoring & Iterative Refinement – During operation the DT continually (a) runs calibration on demand, (b) feeds observed temperatures into the model, and (c) checks safety/optimality metrics; anomalies are flagged using a Kalman filter estimating the unmeasured heatbed temperature. Governance & Evolution – All code, models and calibration scripts are stored in an open‑source GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator) and versioned, allowing the Digital Twin to evolve with changing plant dynamics while preserving a clear audit trail of model updates.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the DT follows a simulation‑driven methodology. Model Construction uses ordinary differential equations derived from the energy balance with four simplifying assumptions. Two models (Model A and Model B) are built; Model B includes an additional heat‑bed inertia term. Calibration & Validation employ a non‑linear least‑squares solver on experimental data collected via the low‑level driver; the calibrated model is validated against a separate data set and visualised in a real‑time dashboard. Integration Architecture uses RabbitMQ as the communication backbone: the controller publishes to and consumes from specific queues, while the low‑level driver streams sensor data and receives actuator commands. Version Control stores all source code (Python scripts for controller, driver, calibration and simulation) and model files in the public GitHub repository for traceability. Evolution Management allows the DT’s calibration routine to be invoked on demand or automatically following detected regime changes, enabling continual refinement of the simulation model as the physical twin ages or incorporates new materials.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is multi‑layered. Real‑time Visualization forwards sensor readings and controller states to a time‑series database (InfluxDB) and displays them on a Grafana dashboard for instant status feedback. Anomaly Detection & State Estimation uses a Kalman filter configured with Model B to fuse measured inside temperature with the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and flag deviations beyond safety thresholds. What‑If Simulation runs co‑simulation scenarios using historical or live PT data to evaluate controller tuning, predict heat‑distribution responses, and optimise controller parameters. Self‑Adaptation Loop – when the Kalman filter reports an anomaly (e.g., due to a new load), the DT automatically schedules an experiment, re‑configures the controller, re‑runs calibration, updates the Kalman filter, executes what‑if optimisations, and finally updates the controller – completing a closed analytic cycle.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Incubator Digital Twin architecture relies on RabbitMQ, a mature lightweight message broker, to mediate all communication between the Raspberry Pi controller, the low‑level driver, and the DT monitoring components. The low‑level driver implements a safety‑oriented protocol that validates incoming commands, only accepts them when the device is in a safe operational state, and uploads sensor readings via the RabbitMQ server. The controller is a bang‑bang logic that enforces temperature limits – it only permits the heatbed to be activated when the measured temperature is below a lower bound (LL) and the temperature remains acceptable for a dwell period (C) after heating. The control logic also ensures a waiting period (H) after heating to avoid overshoot, thereby providing a fail‑safe behaviour. Although explicit cybersecurity mechanisms (such as encryption, authentication or access control) are not described, the architecture includes fundamental safety checks and boundary‑condition enforcement that help mitigate risk of unsafe operation. No explicit data privacy or governance measures are mentioned in the documents.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

directly be controlled.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
